chapters.title: How your body talks back to you
chapters.text:
From childhood, we carry a stubborn belief that health is the most important thing and that everything else can be earned. That phrase makes health sound like manna from heaven, a blessing handed down by some higher power. Yet millions of years of evolution tell a different story. Good health is hard, continuous work: balanced meals, physical effort, a steady routine, and intellectual growth.

The same phrase has another sad flaw. It elevates the physical side of health almost to a sacred level, the way our society has long treated it. Meanwhile, mental health has been downplayed for a long time. Worse, most people are ashamed to admit, even to themselves, that they have a problem. Any psychological, emotional, or moral difficulty we tend to brush off with a soothing phrase: life is what it is, must be hormones, must have been a rough day, I will rest and it will pass. But what if one rough day flows into the next? What if the mood stays after the supposed cause has passed, and rest brings no relief? Those are warning signs, even alarms, your brain rings to say, I feel bad, please help. Here is a list of common warning signs that point to a real psychological struggle.

You may feel detached and indifferent across work, personal life, and relationships. Your productivity may collapse, and you may be stuck in loops of intrusive thoughts that keep you from focusing. Your weight may swing as your eating habits change, with bingeing or refusing food. Your sex drive may disappear. Your sleep may fall apart. Your mood may turn heavy, and you may feel irritation or even suicidal thoughts. You may depend on outside boosters like coffee, alcohol, cigarettes, or sweets just to function. You may feel a steady hum of anxiety.

When any of these signs last more than two weeks, it is time to ask for help. The first person to see on the road to feeling okay is a family doctor. They build a detailed history, an anamnesis, and look for possible sources of the symptoms you have noticed. A depressed mental state can be a side effect of a physical illness, or a physical illness can grow out of a long depressed mental state. The family doctor, drawing on your medical record, assesses your condition and refers you to the right specialist, whether in physical or mental health, and in some cases treatment involves both sides.

It helps to know which mental health professional you are dealing with. A psychiatrist works on tuning up neural networks and neurotransmitters. A psychotherapist helps change deep beliefs, self-image, childhood trauma, and related issues. A psychologist focuses on everyday challenges that are medically neutral, such as relationships, conflicts, values, and career questions.

There is nothing scary about reaching out to a specialist. The stereotypes you see in pop culture about mental illness and being locked up are exaggerated. Forced hospitalization and forced treatment are, in fact, against the law.

In this overview, we will cover the basics of how the brain is built and how it works, understand the biochemistry behind mental struggles, and look at a few examples of mental disorders.

chapters.title: The shared apartment of the brain
chapters.text:
The brain is the body's command center. It regulates every aspect of being human, and every psychological process starts and grows inside it. To understand the mind better, we need a basic look at how the brain works.

The first and most important fact to lock in is that the brain and the body are tightly linked. Changes in one inevitably trigger changes in the other. Physical health depends on mental health, and the reverse is also true.

The brain holds every thought and emotion, but it does not give each one its own room. Joy, anger, euphoria, and sadness all share one large shared apartment. Regulating emotions is the careful, joint work of different brain regions and body signals. The main manager of emotional reactions, the one that acts like a computer, receiving, processing, and sending out information and commands to other parts of the brain and decoding emotions, is the limbic system. The key connector of that system, the part that links body, brain, and emotions, is the hypothalamus. Its multitasking is striking. It handles almost every reactive process in the body, from hunger and fullness to sexual behavior and blood sugar. One of the most interesting roles of the hypothalamus is interoception, the sensing and awareness of what is happening inside your body.

The amygdala is the main transformer and coder that turns outside signals into emotions. It reacts to a trigger first and passes the information on to other structures.

The nervous system as a whole is a complex construct. It splits into two main parts. The central part includes the brain and spinal cord. The peripheral part includes the sympathetic, parasympathetic, and enteric nervous systems. The peripheral system runs a huge number of processes on its own, without direct input from the brain. The sympathetic side handles active reactions and the body's resource distribution. It fires when a person needs to run, fight, or freeze.

People who live with anxiety disorders and stress keep the sympathetic system switched on.

The parasympathetic system is the opposite. It only works in a calm state. But when it is regularly pushed around by an unsettled amygdala and an overactive emotional loop, the body's organs suffer breakdowns. A vivid example of the brain-body link is the diagnosis of VSD, or vegetative-vascular dystonia, which covers any issues with blood pressure regulation and balance in the autonomic nervous system. The paradox is that this diagnosis does not exist in any other country. In practice, it usually means the doctor has no real interest in sorting out the nervous system issues that are causing heart problems.

A small historical note: in ancient Greece, the hypothalamus was thought to be the home of the human soul.

chapters.title: Brain parts in close-up
chapters.text:
The brain and nervous system are made of electrically excitable cells called neurons. Their job is to process and pass on information as either an electrical or a chemical signal. Neurons have short branches called dendrites and long branches called axons. Neurons are picky eaters. To function, they need glucose and ketone bodies, which is why our bodies crave carbohydrates. It is worth noting that neurons are separated from the blood by a selective barrier that blocks most large molecules. As a result, drug treatment is tricky, because the barrier keeps medicines and amino acids away from the neurons.

The places where neurons connect and interact are called synapses. When a neuron charge reaches a synapse, the synapse releases a molecule called a neurotransmitter, which carries the information and tells the next cell what to do. Among the most important neurotransmitters, we should mention glutamate, which drives excitation, dopamine, which the body needs for self-reward, decision making, realistic perception, and control of sexual and eating behavior, and serotonin, the engine of our alertness.

A breakdown in the work and balance of neurotransmitters is part of every mental disorder.

So we can see that the nervous system is a complex, dynamic network that actively develops, learns, and talks to itself.

The way a neural network grows is a lot like the way human relationships grow. Over a lifetime, we build a network of friends and acquaintances. Our connections branch out, and we grow like neurons growing dendrites. With close friends and partners, our connections are active, strong, and lasting, and we keep them going through regular contact, shared interests, and other things. Sometimes, the flow of interaction with a person slowly dries up and the connection fades, usually because we no longer need to talk to them. Neurons work the same way. They keep growing dendrites and linking up with hundreds of other neurons. Yet only the spots that see regular firing and signal passing grow and stay strong. The ones that are not active wither away, and the system reshapes itself.

The fast ability of a neural network to adapt to outside and inside factors is called neuroplasticity. It is what allows humans to adjust to new conditions, to learn, to recover from injury and stress, and more.

chapters.title: Where mental trouble comes from
chapters.text:
There are many possible causes of mental disorders. Darka Ozerna highlights the following.

Genetic predisposition comes from specific versions of receptor genes, called alleles, and from carriers of dopamine and serotonin. It helps to remember that genetics loads the gun, laying down a tendency, while environment and circumstances pull the trigger, awakening and deepening what is already there. So a family history of a particular condition is not a personal sentence. With neuroplasticity, you have the power to rewrite the program.

A difficult childhood is another factor. Children who go through long-term stress in their early years, whether from neglect, abusive parents, or bullying at school, are in a risk group. As with genetics, childhood trauma is not a life sentence, and a qualified specialist can help work through it.

Personality traits also play a role. There is a theory that stress does not appear out of nowhere. It is a side effect of our own traits. Here we are talking about deep, rooted traits, not temporary disorders caused by outside factors. A tendency to discount the positive and to ruminate, to chew over painful thoughts, leads to anhedonia, the inability to feel positive emotions even when there is good reason. Such tendencies can be shaped by society, the flow of information, parents, and life events. One negative event with a strong impact on how a person sees the world can pull a string of other sad situations behind it, set off only by the person bad mood.

Physical health is another factor. As already noted, physical and mental health cannot be separated. Long physical illness can upset the mental balance in two ways. Biologically, it can hurt how neurons interact. And stress-wise, it can wear a person down emotionally.

Poverty is a fifth factor. People with limited financial resources make up one of the largest risk groups for mental disorders. The reasons include poor access to good medical care, poor diet, weak routines and sleep, an unhealthy balance between work and personal life, and dependencies on tobacco, alcohol, and other substances. It is worth noting that most of these factors come from stereotypes that healthy food is expensive and that psychotherapy is for the rich. In reality, that is not true, and rich people get sick too. The task is to challenge these deep beliefs and start improving your own life.

Avocado is pricey, sure, but a boiled egg and peas have a similar nutrient profile and can stand in nicely for it in your diet.

Past trauma is a final factor. Mental trauma does not only come from dramatic events like war, a car crash, or a natural disaster. Any person can carry a traumatic experience from a common situation if its emotional impact crossed the line of what they could bear. You cannot prevent or avoid a traumatic experience, because it does not depend on you. What you can do is be realistic, accept that your bad state is a form of trauma, and start working on it.

chapters.title: Why the brain drags us down and what to do about it
chapters.text:
The most common mental disorder is depression. It shows up so often in our society that it has become almost normal, the way coffee, social media, and TV series are normal.

If you notice the following symptoms in yourself or someone close, and they are not tied to addiction, substance abuse, medication, or a medical condition that affects mood, it is time to act.

You may feel persistently low. You may feel indifferent and unable to feel joy or pleasure. Your weight and appetite may swing. Your sleep may fall apart. You may feel either slowed down or in constant inner unrest. You may feel exhausted, as if the internal battery is at zero. You may struggle to concentrate and to think clearly. And you may have suicidal thoughts.

The trigger for depression is a broken interaction between environment (stress, trauma, diet) and genetics. In people prone to depression, the genes are coded in a way that produces more of the enzymes that break down neurotransmitters. The result is a fault in the neural networks that prevents the brain from working normally. As neurotransmitters break down, the brain runs short of dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and GABA. Without enough serotonin, the anxiety circuits get over-excited, while the pleasure and self-motivation circuits stop responding. More enzymes appear that destroy dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine, and neurons lose GABA, the calming chemical that helps us filter out stimuli and relax.

The symptoms and brain chemistry above describe major depressive disorder, but several other types exist.

Persistent depressive disorder is a low state that lasts at least two years, with periods of full major depression alternating with milder episodes.

Postpartum depression is a serious and dangerous state that carries all the symptoms of major depression and shows up in women after giving birth.

Bipolar disorder is a specific mental illness that combines symptoms of depression with periods of mania and hypomania.

Premenstrual dysphoric disorder, or PMDD, should not be confused with PMS. PMDD is a psychiatric disorder caused by a specific reaction of neurons to sex hormones, including estrogens, progesterone, and androgens. The result is a drop in the neurotransmitters serotonin, dopamine, and GABA that are needed to keep a woman feeling joyful, active, and relaxed. The result is mood swings, irritability, anxiety, and a loss of self-control, among other things.

Between 1.3 and over 5 percent of women suffer from PMDD.

Seasonal affective disorder, or SAD, is a recurring depression that activates in a particular season. It comes from an imbalance of melatonin and serotonin, the brain slows down, and a permanent sleepiness sets in. SAD is not seen as a separate disease, only as a feature of major depression or bipolar disorder.

The good news is that all forms of depression respond to treatment. The main options are antidepressants, drugs that use chemical compounds to help tune up brain function, psychotherapy, including cognitive-behavioral, interpersonal, and solution-focused approaches, brain stimulation, which can be transcranial, magnetic, vagus nerve, or electroconvulsive, and lifestyle changes, including physical activity, balanced eating, social time, and cutting back on harmful habits.

chapters.title: Heads up. Anxiety alert
chapters.text:
As hard as it is to admit, depression is far from the only popular mental disorder of our time. Right next to it on the leaderboard sits anxiety and stress-related disorder, and its many types. They have become especially relevant in light of recent world events, pandemic outbreaks, political and armed conflicts, and even full-scale wars. Let us look at them more closely.

Worrying and feeling stressed have become normal today, because the triggers are many. However, when anxiety does not fade after the threat is gone, or when it shows up without a clear reason, it is worth a closer look. In the brain, anxiety gets switched on by the amygdala in response to a threatening trigger. The brain takes in what it knows, processes the data, and decides what to do. With a small danger, a healthy brain finds a quick, simple solution and calms down. An anxious brain, with an oversensitive and fearful amygdala, blows the threat out of proportion, breaks free of the prefrontal cortex, and tumbles into endless catastrophic fantasies. The result of long-term anxiety is a loss of neuroplasticity, damage to neurons in the hippocampus, and a fading of positive memories. People with anxiety see the world through catastrophically tinted glasses.

Beyond the constant inner unrest, anxiety also hits the digestive system and can trigger a range of stomach and gut symptoms.

Like depression, anxiety disorder comes in several forms. Here are the most common.

Generalized anxiety disorder is anxiety caused by the inability to control ordinary daily things. Specific phobia is an irrational fear of particular objects or situations. Social anxiety disorder is the fear of social judgment and isolation. Panic disorder brings regular attacks of intense fear. Illness anxiety disorder is excessive, inappropriate worry about your own health. Obsessive-compulsive disorder, or OCD, is the need to repeat a ritual of actions to keep intrusive thoughts at bay. Substance- or medication-induced anxiety disorder is anxiety that comes from taking certain medications and fades when the medication is stopped. Adjustment disorder with anxiety is a sharp negative reaction to new conditions and circumstances a person is not used to.

In the context of recent events in Ukraine, it is worth looking more closely at post-traumatic stress disorder, or PTSD. It develops as a result of deeply traumatic events. In witnesses of war, violence, or death, intense stress disrupts the endocrine system and reprograms neural networks, so these structures lose the ability to assess threats and react to them properly. Anxiety rises through an imbalance in hormones and neurotransmitters. Dopamine and norepinephrine climb, while serotonin and GABA drop. The brain, faced with even the smallest threat, fires the same old response, a rush of horrible memories and associations. The terrible images of the past keep surfacing in the person's mind and get in the way of normal life and function.

To work through PTSD and other forms of anxiety, the first step is to see a specialist, get a diagnosis, and find out whether other psychiatric or physical conditions are also present. If a course of antidepressants is prescribed, it should be taken, and where possible, cognitive-behavioral therapy should be added. It is also worth exploring self-help options and starting to work on improving your own state.

chapters.title: The relationship with food
chapters.text:
After reading the above, you have probably noticed that a disturbed relationship with food is one of the key symptoms of mental disorders, and that fixing that relationship is one of the key steps on the road to recovery. After all, the food we eat is what supplies our bodies with the substances they need.

In this section, we will talk about mental disorders that center on a broken relationship with food, namely anorexia and bulimia.

A person with anorexia has a dangerously low body mass index, meaning a weight that is extremely low for their age and height. It is usually caused by severe food restriction, abuse of laxatives, and excessive exercise.

Bulimia, though similar to anorexia, is marked not by restriction but by repeated binge eating followed by an act of preventing weight gain, which is purging. It is often paired with other compulsive behaviors, including shopping, cooking, drug and alcohol abuse, and self-harm.

Because of how their brains are wired, people with anorexia, whose reward system does not push them to consume, are not prone to alcohol or drug addiction.

Despite the common belief that anorexia and bulimia are triggered by unhealthy beauty standards and the demands of a patriarchal society, researchers have concluded that the main factors in these conditions are heredity, with the illness often appearing in teenagers, which suggests that puberty activates the genes that shape eating behavior, poorly developed interoception, meaning the person has a hard time reading their body, for instance, not feeling hunger or fullness, and the inability to tolerate certain emotional states, which flips on a defense mechanism to block the unpleasant feeling, either by refusing food or by compulsive eating and stress eating.

People with anorexia tend to be quite withdrawn and isolated, drawn to asceticism, anxious, and unable to feel joy. Their brains show very low sensitivity to reward and almost no pleasure from food. People with this illness often hide their condition and find it very hard to admit they have a problem. Anorexia is deeply rooted in the brain's neural networks, which makes it hard to fight. Bulimia, which also tends to activate in the teen years, is usually triggered by parental neglect, when parents fail to give the child enough care and protection, which is a dose of oxytocin. As a result, the neural networks of the limbic system form in the wrong way, and that later shows up as an inability to self-regulate and stay in control.

Both illnesses take a serious toll on overall health, damage the body, and can lead to many related conditions.

To work through these disorders, a combined approach is needed, covering both the mental and the physical. There must be a gradual return to normal eating, healing of the digestive system, sessions with a psychotherapist, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and work on the problem with the support of family and close friends.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Summing up, we can say with confidence that mental health is not dark matter. Scientific progress has made this branch of medicine as treatable and adjustable as any other part of the body. The main takeaway is that a mental disorder is not a life sentence. Sometimes it is okay to be not okay, and the important thing is to understand that something is off and that help is needed. Beyond that, the old scary stories about chemicals and so-called dangerous drugs, meaning antidepressants, have long since worn thin and do not reflect reality. Modern medicines are not shooting in the dark. They are aimed at fixing broken neurotransmitter communication, raising or lowering their levels, and resetting the receptors that accept them. Medication is also not mandatory. In many cases, sessions with a psychotherapist, dietary changes, and a healthier lifestyle can improve the situation without any drugs. It is also worth saying that people with mental disorders are not monsters, not crazy, and not hopeless. A depressive episode, anxiety, or eating problems can happen to almost anyone, and a supportive circle, not a rejecting one, is one of the deciding factors on the road to recovery.

The last and probably most important point is the role of mental health in the current reality. For example, Ukraine has been dealt a heavy hand of historical and cultural trauma. Ukrainians were killed, invaded, starved, their language and culture banned, and even their history rewritten. Yet, despite all that, they survived. Somewhere deep in the genes, the pain and suffering of past generations is etched. But along with it runs an unbreakable spirit. So not only Ukrainians, but every people, should work through their historical and cultural traumas at a national level, not from the position of a suffering victim, but from the position of a fighter who survived. Then we will all become a little healthier and a little more aware.

A few tips. Be attentive and kind to yourself and your loved ones. Watch for shifts in mood, ideas, and narratives that show up in your circle of friends and family. If you spot warning signs, help the person, who may not even realize they are going through a hard time.

Worth reading from start to finish?
